meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a Motion Canvas scene architecture decision is right or wrong.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: One scene should carry one argument.
logic: Programmatic video can easily become a dumping ground.
Viewers retain transformations better than lists.
A clear scene goal enables reusable code and strong editing.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before coding, write the scene sentence: This scene proves that __.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Motion Canvas code must be skimmable as a storyboard by someone who knows the domain but not the codebase.
logic: Programmatic animation is unique: the code is the source of truth for timing, layout, and visuals simultaneously.
If timing logic is embedded inside layout calculations, neither can be reviewed independently.
Separation of concerns — signals, layout, animation — enables parallel review: a designer reviews timing, an engineer reviews layout, both review the same file structure.
Without this separation, every change to timing risks breaking layout, and every layout adjustment risks breaking the animation sequence.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Structure every scene with three sections: (1) signals declared at the top with semantic names, (2) layout as a pure function of signals, (3) animation as a generator that only modifies signals. Add comments for each visual beat with approximate timestamps. The file should read like a storyboard: 'Scene 3: How the cache improves response time. Beat 1 (0-600ms): server node appears...'

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: A technical explainer must communicate its core logic with sound muted. If the animation requires narration to make sense, the visual design has failed.
logic: Narration explains. Animation proves. When animation merely illustrates narration, it's redundant — a static diagram with voiceover would be equally effective.
The unique value of animation is showing transformation, cause-and-effect, and state change over time.
If the viewer can watch silently and understand the argument (even if they miss nuance), the animation is doing its job.
If the viewer is lost without the voiceover, the animation is decoration, not communication.
reasoning_chains.so_what: For every scene, review once with sound muted. Can you follow the argument? If not, the visual hierarchy needs restructuring. The highest standard: someone who is not an expert in the domain should be able to describe what happened after watching silently.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Motion Canvas components should be as disciplined as React components: one responsibility, clean props interface, no hidden signal dependencies.
logic: Without component discipline, Motion Canvas projects become monolithic — the project you made for one explainer cannot be adapted for the next.
Reusable components (Node, Arrow, Label, Chart) compound in value: the second explainer takes half the time, the fifth takes 20% of the time.
Signals passed as props create explicit data flow. Signals referenced from global scope create hidden coupling.
A component library transforms Motion Canvas from a 'per-video script' tool into a 'video production system' tool.
reasoning_chains.so_what: After completing any explainer, extract the 3 most generic visual components into a shared components/ folder. Give each component a clear interface: what signals it accepts, what it renders, what it does not animate internally. The next project starts by importing these components — not by rewriting them.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Every scene needs a rhythm — a deliberate alternation of motion and stillness. Scenes without holds feel rushed; scenes without motion feel static.
logic: Motion Canvas makes continuous motion easy — tweens chain naturally, signals update smoothly.
But continuous motion without stillness is exhausting to watch. The viewer's attention system needs recovery periods.
A scene rhythm pattern: motion (establishes the idea, 600-1200ms) → hold (lets it land, 500-1000ms) → motion (shows consequence, 600-1200ms) → hold (prepares for transition, 500-800ms).
The hold before the scene transition is particularly important — it signals closure and prepares the viewer for a topic shift.
reasoning_chains.so_what: In every scene's animation generator, insert explicit waitFor() calls between beats. Name the waits: yield* waitFor(0.8); // Viewer absorbs the comparison. Don't let tweens chain continuously without intentional pauses. The scene duration should include holds — a 3-beat scene with holds might be 5-7 seconds, and that's correct.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Every visual beat in a scene must be explicitly named, numbered, and time-budgeted before coding begins.
logic: Without beat planning, scenes grow organically: 'let me just add one more thing.' The scene drifts from 3 beats to 7, and the argument gets diluted.
Named beats serve as a contract: the scene will communicate exactly these ideas in this order. If a new idea is important, it becomes a new scene — not a squeezed-in beat.
Numbered beats enable review: 'Beat 2 was unclear. Beat 3 was strong.' Without numbers, feedback is vague: 'the middle part was confusing.'
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before writing any Motion Canvas code, write a beat list as comments: // Beat 1 (0-600ms): Database node appears, label 'PostgreSQL'. // Beat 2 (600-1400ms): Query arrow travels to API node. // Beat 3 (1400-2200ms): Response returns, data transforms visually. Each beat gets a time budget and a single sentence. If a beat needs two sentences, split it into two beats or a new scene.
